items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: Why do glaciated valleys have a U-shape rather than a V-shape?
items.correct: Glaciers occupy the whole valley width, eroding the sides as well as the floor, producing steep sides and a flat floor (U-shape); rivers erode mainly downwards, leaving V-shape.
items.misconception: Glaciers cut U-shapes because ice is shaped like a U.
items.random_wrong: Glaciers carve V-shaped valleys just like rivers do.
items.why: The U-shape reflects the geometry of erosion (lateral plus vertical), not the shape of the ice itself. A river is confined to a thin channel and erodes vertically — a glacier fills the valley.

items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: It's Sahel. “Maghreb” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 250mm. “100mm” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
